section.title: Authority and the Parable of the Tenants
section.sectionRationale: The leaders challenge Jesus' authority, and He answers with a counter-question and two vineyard parables. The two sons expose a religion of words without obedience; the wicked tenants indict leaders who reject God's messengers and will kill His Son, and announce that the kingdom will be given to a fruit-bearing people.
section.thesis: Jesus' authority is from heaven, and the leaders who reject it prove, like the disobedient son and the murderous tenants, to be fruitless and self-condemned; the rejected Son is God's cornerstone, and the kingdom is given to a people who bear its fruit.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the challenge to Jesus' authority and the question about John; the parable of the two sons; and the parable of the wicked tenants with the rejected cornerstone.

verses.text: When Jesus returned to the temple courts and began to teach, the chief priests and elders of the people came up to Him. “By what {{exousia|authority}} are You doing these things?” they asked. “And who gave You this authority?”
“I will also ask you one question,” Jesus replied, “and if you answer Me, I will tell you by what authority I am doing these things.
What was the source of John’s baptism? Was it from heaven or from men?” They deliberated among themselves and said, “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ He will ask, ‘Why then did you not believe him?’
But if we say, ‘From men,’ we are afraid of the people, for they all regard John as a prophet.”
So they answered, “We do not know.” And Jesus replied, “Neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things.

units.label: The parable of the two sons

verses.text: But what do you think? There was a man who had two sons. He went to the first one and said, ‘Son, go and work today in the vineyard.’
‘I will not,’ he replied. But later he changed his mind and went.
Then the man went to the second son and told him the same thing. ‘I will, sir,’ he said. But he did not go.
Which of the two did the will of his father?” “The first,” they answered. Jesus said to them, “Truly I tell you, the tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the kingdom of God before you.
For John came to you in the way of righteousness, and you did not believe him, but the tax collectors and prostitutes did. And even after you saw this, you did not repent and believe him.

Listen to another parable: There was a landowner who planted a vineyard. He put a wall around it, dug a winepress in it, and built a tower. Then he rented it out to some tenants and went away on a journey.
When the harvest time drew near, he sent his servants to the tenants to collect his share of the fruit.
But the tenants seized his servants. They beat one, killed another, and stoned a third.
Again, he sent other servants, more than the first group. But the tenants did the same to them.
Finally, he sent his son to them. ‘They will respect my son,’ he said.
But when the tenants saw the son, they said to one another, ‘This is the heir. Come, let us kill him and take his inheritance.’
So they seized him and threw him out of the vineyard and killed him.
Therefore, when the owner of the vineyard returns, what will he do to those tenants?”
“He will bring those wretches to a wretched end,” they replied, “and will rent out the vineyard to other tenants who will give him his share of the fruit at harvest time.”
Jesus said to them, “Have you never read in the Scriptures: ‘The {{lithos|stone}} the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. This is from the Lord, and it is marvelous in our eyes’?
Therefore I tell you that the kingdom of God will be taken away from you and given to a people who will produce its {{karpos|fruit}}.
He who falls on this stone will be broken to pieces, but he on whom it falls will be crushed.”
When the chief priests and Pharisees heard His parables, they knew that Jesus was speaking about them.
Although they wanted to arrest Him, they were afraid of the crowds, because the people regarded Him as a prophet.

exousia.gloss: Authority, the right and power to act. The leaders demand the source of Jesus' authority; He exposes that they will not honestly reckon with heaven's authority in John, and so forfeit the right to His answer. His authority is from the Father.

lithos.gloss: A stone. Quoting Psalm 118, Jesus is the stone the builders rejected that becomes the cornerstone; the very Son the tenants kill is the foundation of God's new building, and the stone that saves also judges those who stumble on it.

karpos.gloss: Fruit, produce, the yield the owner rightly seeks. The vineyard was to give its fruit to God; because the tenants withhold it, the kingdom is given to a people who will produce its fruit, the mark of true belonging to God.

basic.context: Back in the temple, the chief priests and elders demand to know by what authority Jesus acts. He answers with a question about John's baptism they will not answer honestly, so He declines to answer them. Then He tells two parables. In the first, one son says no but obeys, another says yes but does not, and the leaders, like the second, are outdone by tax collectors and prostitutes who repented. In the second, tenants beat and kill the owner's servants and finally his son; the kingdom, Jesus says, will be taken from them and given to a fruit-bearing people, for the rejected stone is now the cornerstone.
basic.hermeneutics: These are confrontation parables aimed squarely at the leaders, who understand that Jesus means them. The vineyard is Israel, the servants are the prophets, the son is Jesus, and the tenants are the current leadership. The point is not merit but response: honest repentance and fruit versus a religion of words. The cornerstone quotation reframes Jesus' coming rejection as God's plan.
basic.originalLanguages: The leaders demand the source of Jesus' exousia, authority, but forfeit His answer. Jesus is the lithos, the stone, rejected by the builders yet made the cornerstone. And the kingdom goes to a people who produce its karpos, its fruit.
basic.typology: The vineyard fulfills Isaiah 5, God's vineyard Israel. The rejected stone become cornerstone is Psalm 118, fulfilled in Christ crucified and risen. The killed son foreshadows the cross itself.
basic.christ: Jesus is the beloved Son sent last of all, rejected and killed outside the vineyard, yet raised to become the cornerstone of God's new building. He is the authority the leaders will not own and the stone on which people are either built or broken.
basic.god: God is the patient owner who sends servant after servant and at last His own Son, and the just judge who will remove the kingdom from fruitless stewards and give it to those who bear fruit. He turns the rejected Son into the cornerstone.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 5 is the song of the vineyard behind the parable. Psalm 118 gives the rejected stone. Acts 4 applies the cornerstone to Christ before these same leaders. 1 Peter 2 develops the stone that saves and stumbles.
basic.oneStory: In the temple He came to claim, Jesus exposes the leaders who reject heaven's authority, and in two parables He unveils the whole story: God's vineyard, His prophets rejected, His Son sent and killed, and the kingdom given to a fruit-bearing people. The rejected Son becomes the cornerstone. The plot of the passion is laid bare, and the leaders, understanding, only harden, unable to arrest Him yet because of the crowds.

context.literary: These controversies fill Jesus' last days of public teaching in the temple, between the entry and the Olivet Discourse. The authority question sets up three parables of judgment, the two sons, the tenants, and, in the next chapter, the wedding banquet, each exposing the leaders' rejection. The tenants parable draws directly on Isaiah 5 and points forward to Jesus' death and the transfer of the kingdom.
context.historical: John the Baptist was widely revered as a prophet, which traps the leaders publicly. Absentee landowners and tenant farmers were common in Galilee, and disputes over the harvest could turn violent. Killing an heir to claim vacant land reflects real legal maneuvering. The cornerstone or capstone was the crucial stone that aligned or crowned a building.
context.canonical: The vineyard is Israel from Isaiah 5:1-7. The rejected-stone cornerstone is Psalm 118:22-23, applied to Christ in Acts 4:11 and 1 Peter 2:4-8. The transfer of the kingdom to a fruit-bearing people anticipates the ingathering of Jew and Gentile in the church, the new-covenant people who bear fruit, John 15:1-8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To vindicate Jesus' divine authority, to expose the leaders' fruitless, unbelieving religion against the repentance of outcasts, and to foretell the rejection and vindication of the Son and the giving of the kingdom to a fruit-bearing people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables describe the leaders' guilt and God's judgment, and they foretell the passion. They prescribe, for every hearer, genuine repentance and fruit over mere profession, and warn of the peril of rejecting the cornerstone.

debates.question: Who is the people to whom the kingdom is given in verse 43?

views.label: The church of Jew and Gentile
views.case: The kingdom passes from the current unfaithful leadership to the new-covenant people gathered by Jesus, in which believing Jews and Gentiles together bear the fruit God seeks, fulfilling the promise that in Abraham's seed all nations are blessed.

views.label: A faithful, fruit-bearing remnant and new leadership
views.case: The emphasis falls on producing fruit rather than on ethnicity; the kingdom is taken from fruitless stewards and given to those, including repentant Israelites like the apostles, who yield God His fruit, so the point is fruitfulness, not simple replacement of one nation by another.

debates.bearing: Both agree the decisive issue is bearing fruit for God, not privilege or position; the kingdom belongs to the people God gathers in Christ who yield Him the fruit the old stewards withheld.

study.originalLanguages: The leaders demand to know the source of Jesus' exousia, His authority, but their refusal to judge John honestly shows they will not submit to heaven's authority, and so they forfeit His answer. In the parables Jesus lays bare the story: God's vineyard, His servants the prophets beaten and killed, and at last His Son. That Son is the lithos, the stone, that the builders reject, yet God makes Him the cornerstone, so that the very act of rejection becomes the foundation of a new building. Because the tenants withhold the karpos, the fruit, the kingdom is given to a people who will produce it, for fruitfulness, not position, marks those who truly belong to God.

typology.fulfillment: Israel as God's planting in Isaiah 5, now under judgment for withholding its fruit.

typology.type: The son sent last and killed
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the beloved Son, rejected and crucified outside the city, Hebrews 13:12.
The crucified and risen Christ, the foundation of God's new building, Psalm 118:22, Acts 4:11.

study.christ: Jesus is the Son of the vineyard's owner, sent last of all after the prophets, and the parable foretells His own death: seized, thrown out of the vineyard, and killed. Yet He is also the stone the builders rejected that God makes the cornerstone, so that His rejection becomes the foundation of a new people. His authority, which the leaders will not acknowledge, is the Father's own. He is the stone on which all must reckon: those who come to Him in faith are built up, while those who stumble over Him in unbelief are broken. In Him the kingdom passes to all who bear the fruit God seeks.
study.god: God is the patient and long-suffering owner who plants a vineyard, provides for it, and sends messenger after messenger to seek its fruit, absorbing rejection and even the murder of His servants before sending His own Son. He is also the righteous judge who will not leave fruitless stewards in charge forever; He removes the kingdom from those who withhold His due and gives it to a people who will yield Him fruit. And He is the God of surprising reversal, who takes the stone men reject and makes it the cornerstone, so that His purpose triumphs through the very rejection of His Son.

crossReferences.note: The song of the vineyard, God's people who yield wild grapes, behind the tenants parable.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, quoted by Jesus.
Peter applies the cornerstone to the crucified and risen Christ before these same leaders.
Christ the living stone, precious to believers but a stone of stumbling to the disobedient.

study.oneStory: In the temple He came to claim, Jesus lays bare the whole story of God's dealings: a vineyard planted, prophets sent and rejected, the Son sent and killed, and the kingdom given to a fruit-bearing people. The leaders understand that He means them, yet only harden. The rejected Son becomes the cornerstone, and His coming death is revealed as God's plan, not His defeat. The passion narrative now gathers momentum toward the cross, where the stone the builders reject will become the foundation of a new people gathered from every nation to bear God's fruit.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond to the demand to know His authority?

options.text: He asked whether John's baptism was from heaven, exposing their refusal to reckon honestly with the truth.
options.note: Verses 24-27 show Jesus' counter-question unmasking their bad faith.

options.text: He refused to answer and said nothing further.
options.note: He did decline to answer directly, but only after exposing them with a probing question, which this omits.

options.text: He immediately claimed to be the Son of God.
options.note: He revealed His identity through parables here rather than a direct claim to them.

options.text: He admitted He had no authority to act.
options.note: His whole response assumes His authority is from heaven.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the two sons teach?

options.text: Real obedience, not mere words, does the Father's will, so repentant outcasts enter ahead of those who only profess.
options.note: Verses 28-32 contrast saying and doing, exalting repentance.

options.text: It is better to say yes to God even if you do not follow through.
options.note: The opposite; the son who said yes but did nothing failed to do the Father's will.

options.text: Both sons pleased the father equally in the end.
options.note: Only the first, who actually went, did the father's will.

options.text: God cares only about outward words of commitment.
options.note: The parable prizes doing over merely saying.

quiz.q: In the parable of the tenants, who is the son who is killed?

options.text: Jesus, the beloved Son sent last, rejected and killed, foretelling His own death.
options.note: The son sent after the servants pictures Jesus and His coming crucifixion.

options.text: One of the prophets sent to Israel.
options.note: The prophets are the earlier servants; the son is distinct and greater, pointing to Jesus.

options.text: John the Baptist, the last messenger before Jesus.
options.note: John is among the messengers, but the son is the owner's own heir, Jesus Himself.

options.text: A symbol with no particular fulfillment.
options.note: Jesus clearly intends the son to represent Himself, as the leaders perceive.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean that the kingdom will be given to a people who produce its fruit?

options.text: It is taken from the fruitless stewards and given to the fruit-bearing people God gathers in Christ.
options.note: Verse 43 ties belonging to the kingdom to bearing fruit for God.

options.text: God will replace the leaders with better human rulers.
options.note: New leadership is involved, but the point is a fruit-bearing people, not merely swapping rulers.

options.text: The kingdom depends on producing enough religious works.
options.note: The fruit flows from genuine faith and repentance, not mere works to earn the kingdom.

options.text: God has abandoned the idea of a kingdom altogether.
options.note: The kingdom continues, given to those who bear its fruit.

questions: The leaders would not answer honestly about John because it cost them too much. Where are you avoiding a truth about Jesus because of what it would require?
One son said no but obeyed, the other said yes but did not. Which son are you more like right now, and where is the gap between your words and your obedience?
Tax collectors and prostitutes entered ahead of the respectable. How does that unsettle any confidence you place in your religious standing rather than in repentance?
The owner kept sending messengers and finally his Son. How have you responded to God's patient pursuit of you, and to the Son He sent?
The stone men rejected became the cornerstone. Is Jesus the foundation you are building on, or a stone you keep stumbling over?

translationNotes.note: Manuscripts vary in the order of the two sons and in which answer the leaders give; in every form the point is unchanged, that doing the father's will, not merely saying yes, is what counts.
The quotation is Psalm 118:22-23; the word rendered cornerstone is literally the head of the corner, the crucial stone of a building, applied to the rejected and risen Christ.
A few early manuscripts do not include verse 44, which parallels Luke 20:18; it is retained here as in the BSB and pictures the stone that both saves and judges.

Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
